items.headline: ALJ Bars Livestream from June 29 Hearing; DEA Resists Testimony from Its Own Official on Marijuana Harms
items.summary: Chief Administrative Law Judge Derek Julius's June 18, 2026 Preliminary Order bars all recording devices from the hearing room and lobby — the June 29 broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing will not be livestreamed. In-person attendance at DEA's 700 Army Navy Drive, Arlington, VA facility is the only way to observe the proceedings. Marijuana Moment counsel formally requested reconsideration on June 23, arguing the DEA had allowed livestreaming of the prior (Biden-era) cancelled hearing; Americans for Safe Access and partner advocates sent a separate letter; as of June 25-26 no reversal had been issued by Chief ALJ Julius. In a separate procedural development reported by Marijuana Moment on June 25, DEA resisted testimony from a DEA agency official linked to a report on marijuana's harms — an unusual stance given the agency presents as the first witness on June 29 as proponent of the rescheduling rule.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma cultivators and dispensaries monitoring the June 29–July 15 hearing — which addresses only adult-use rescheduling and does not affect the already-effective Schedule III status of state-licensed medical marijuana — cannot follow proceedings remotely. The DEA's resistance to its own official's testimony on marijuana's harms is a notable procedural signal: the agency is managing what enters the administrative record even on the government's own side. Chief ALJ Julius's scope language (June 19) already confirms the hearing does not touch OMMA operators' current Schedule III status, 280E relief, or registration obligation; these procedural developments reinforce that the hearing is likely to produce a selective administrative record, which will matter more for D.C. Circuit litigation risk than for Oklahoma operators' immediate compliance choices.

items.headline: SAFER Banking Act Reintroduced with Bipartisan Support in Senate and House — June 25, 2026
items.summary: Bipartisan lawmakers reintroduced the SAFER Banking Act in both chambers on June 25, 2026, the eve of the DEA registration deadline. Senate sponsors: Sens. Jeff Merkley (D-OR), Lisa Murkowski (R-AK), Elizabeth Warren (D-MA), and Steve Daines (R-MT). House sponsors: Reps. Jim Himes (D-CT), Warren Davidson (R-OH), Nydia Velázquez (D-NY), Brian Mast (R-FL), Lou Correa (D-CA), Guy Reschenthaler (R-PA), and Dina Titus (D-NV). The 2026 version would bar federal regulators from penalizing financial institutions for banking state-legal cannabis businesses, and explicitly extends safe harbor to ancillary businesses including landlords, technology vendors, accounting firms, and security providers.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma dispensaries and cultivators continue to face banking access challenges — accounts closed without warning, cash-only operations, and limited payment processing. The SAFER Banking Act has passed the House multiple times but has stalled in the Senate. Reintroduction is not enactment; operators should not change banking arrangements based on a bill filing alone. However, the bipartisan co-sponsors and timing alongside Schedule III rescheduling may give the bill better prospects than prior sessions.

items.headline: Rescheduling Opponents File Pre-Hearing Briefs June 26 — Nebraska/Idaho/Indiana: Marijuana Causes Psychiatric Harm; Dr. D'Souza to Testify; Louisiana Dropped from D.C. Circuit
items.summary: On June 26, parties opposing marijuana rescheduling filed prehearing statements with the DEA ALJ ahead of the June 29 broader-rescheduling hearing. The states of Nebraska, Idaho, and Indiana filed a joint statement arguing marijuana 'causes our citizens psychiatric harm and is linked to increases in homelessness, traffic accidents, illegal drug trafficking, and other crime.' The states designated Dr. Deepak Cyril D'Souza — inaugural director of the Yale Center for the Science of Cannabis and Cannabinoids — as their expert witness on abuse liability. Separately, the June 26 D.C. Circuit briefing deadline was met: the states filed their opening brief on that schedule. Louisiana AG Liz Murrill had previously sought dismissal from the D.C. Circuit case (May 29), leaving only Nebraska and Indiana as state-AG petitioners. The government (DOJ/DEA) response brief is due July 2, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: The prehearing arguments confirm the administrative record at the June 29–July 15 hearing will be built entirely around psychiatric harm, criminal justice, and traffic safety data — no commercial, patient, or agricultural perspective will be in the record. For Oklahoma OMMA operators, this does not affect the already-effective Schedule III status for state-licensed medical marijuana (Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 order explicitly excluded this from the hearing scope). However, the D.C. Circuit briefing is now complete on the petitioner side; the government's July 2 response and any subsequent stay ruling represent the continued risk that the April 28 order could be halted. Harris Sliwoski's Canna Law Blog noted a one-sided administrative record may weaken the final rule's durability under judicial review.

items.headline: DEA 60-Day Expedited Registration Window Officially Closes June 29; Broader-Rescheduling ALJ Hearing Opens as Scheduled — No Stay Granted
items.summary: The DEA's 60-day expedited registration window for Schedule III medical marijuana operators officially closes June 29, 2026 — the effective final day under the Saturday-rule shift (60th calendar day was June 27; confirmed by Manatt, Phelps & Phillips). Operators who filed Form 225 with drug codes 7362/7353/7386 by today hold safe-harbor operating authority during DEA's six-month review. The dedicated dispensary portal had closed June 22; no dedicated manufacturer/cultivator/lab/distributor portal launched before the window closed — Form 225 was the only available pathway. Simultaneously, the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing opened as scheduled at 9:00 a.m. ET at DEA's 700 Army Navy Drive Arlington VA facility; no emergency injunction or stay was granted by any federal court as of June 29. The government (DOJ/DEA) is testifying first on Day 1 per Chief ALJ Julius's June 24 testimony schedule. Operators who applied may still expect an on-site DEA inspection as part of the review process — consistent with the June 19 Mississippi dispensary visits.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma cultivators, processors, labs, and distributors who did not file Form 225 by June 29 have MISSED the 60-day expedited window and have no safe-harbor operating authority while a late application is reviewed. OBNDD's January 1, 2027 enforcement grace period is not a substitute — it only delays when OBNDD begins active enforcement, not whether DEA's safe-harbor applies during review. Operators can still apply outside the expedited window at deadiversion.usdoj.gov — late filing is better than not filing, but carries no safe-harbor guarantee. For Oklahoma dispensaries, OMMA registration remains optional (no OBNDD mandate applies to dispensaries). The ALJ hearing is confirmed underway; its scope is ONLY adult-use/recreational marijuana — the April 28 Schedule III order for state-licensed medical marijuana is not at issue per Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 scope order.

items.headline: Trulieve Becomes First U.S. Cannabis Company to List on NYSE — Schedule III Rescheduling Removes Major Exchange Barrier
items.summary: Trulieve Cannabis Corp. announced June 5, 2026 that its shares were approved for listing on the New York Stock Exchange; the stock began trading under ticker TRLV on June 10, 2026, making Trulieve the first U.S. plant-touching cannabis company to list on a major U.S. exchange. The listing followed the April 28 rescheduling of state-licensed medical marijuana to Schedule III, which removed the prior regulatory barrier that major U.S. exchanges had cited in refusing to list cannabis operators. Trulieve also executed a corporate restructuring separating its medical operations from mixed-use activities. Curaleaf Holdings and Verano Holdings separately executed reverse stock splits (1-for-3 and 1-for-5 respectively) as steps toward potential uplistings on major exchanges.
items.whyItMatters: Trulieve's NYSE listing is the first direct capital-markets consequence of Schedule III rescheduling — a major exchange has made an affirmative determination that Schedule III status is sufficient to permit listing U.S. plant-touching operators. For CAKE and Oklahoma wholesale dispensary customers, NYSE access matters because it lowers the cost of capital for large multistate operators, which may accelerate national consolidation and put further wholesale pricing pressure on smaller-state operators. Oklahoma operators remain state-licensed only and are not directly NYSE-eligible under current rules, but the shift signals that the equity capital environment for cannabis operators has changed materially since April 28. The listing also serves as a market-level signal that the Schedule III order has sufficient durability that a major exchange — with its own regulatory and reputational exposure — was willing to accept the listing.

source.name: MJBizDaily — Trulieve to become first US cannabis company on NYSE

items.headline: Oklahoma HB 2837 — All Medical Marijuana Employees Must Complete Annual OMMA Training Credential; Effective January 1, 2027
items.summary: Oklahoma HB 2837 became law without Governor Stitt's signature on May 27, 2025. Beginning January 1, 2027, all employees of OMMA-licensed businesses must annually complete an educational training course provided or approved by OMMA and submit proof of completion in their OMMA employee credential application; employees must also pass a fingerprint-based background check through a third-party vendor contracted by OMMA. Training must cover state statutes and administrative rules, patient privacy requirements, and safe handling and storage of medical marijuana. Preliminary cost estimates range $250–$500 per employee per annual training cycle. OMMA is still promulgating implementing rules for the approved-training program.
items.whyItMatters: All employees across every OMMA license type — cultivators, processors, dispensaries, laboratories, and distributors — must obtain the new OMMA-approved training credential by January 1, 2027, now less than seven months away. Dispensaries with high turnover and cultivators with seasonal labor face compounding training costs and ongoing credential tracking obligations. OMMA is expected to include training documentation as a checklist item during inspections and audits. Licensed businesses should identify or confirm OMMA-approved training programs now — implementing rules delays could create a compliance crunch close to the effective date. Failure to credential employees by January 1, 2027 exposes the business to OMMA enforcement action.

items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 1 (June 29): Government's FDA Witness Testifies Marijuana Safer Than Alcohol and Opioids — SAM Calls Rescheduling 'Solely Political'
items.summary: The broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing opened June 29 at 9:00 a.m. ET at DEA headquarters in Arlington, VA, with the government (DOJ/DEA) presenting as the proponent of the rule. FDA's Dominic Chiapperino — Director of the Controlled Substance Staff at the Center for Drug Evaluation and Research — testified to the eight-factor scheduling analysis, finding marijuana's abuse potential 'less than Schedule I/II substances.' He compared cannabis withdrawal (irritability-level) favorably to alcohol (which carries seizure risk and death-level withdrawal syndrome) and opioids — the government's own affirmative scientific case for Schedule III classification. Dr. Corey Burchman (board-certified pain management physician, NH) began his testimony on Day 2 (June 30), describing how he has transitioned patients from opioids to marijuana. SAM CEO Kevin Sabet (a designated participant scheduled to testify July 6) issued a press statement at an event outside the hearing calling rescheduling 'solely political,' citing cannabis industry campaign donations to Trump. Pro-reform advocates denied participation held a press conference outside the facility. No emergency stay or injunction was granted. The hearing has no livestream; DEA.gov transcript publishes only after proceedings conclude post-July 15.
items.whyItMatters: The government's own FDA scientific witness presented affirmative evidence for Schedule III classification — this is the medical case the government will defend in the D.C. Circuit. Sabet's 'solely political' framing will circulate heavily through July 15 as designated anti-rescheduling participants testify one by one. The hearing information blackout (no livestream, no daily transcript) means any social media or newsletter claims about 'what was revealed today' between July 1 and July 15 are unverifiable until DEA releases the official transcript. Oklahoma operators should not adjust compliance posture based on unverified hearing-day claims. Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 scope order confirmed the hearing addresses only adult-use marijuana — it does not affect Oklahoma state-licensed medical marijuana's Schedule III status or OBNDD registration obligation.

items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 2 (June 30): Opponents Attack Government's CAMU Methodology — Five-to-Two-Part Test Switch Introduced 2 Months Before FDA Finished Analysis; Burchman Testifies Marijuana Replaced Opioids for Pain Patients
items.summary: On the second day of the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing (June 30, 2026), two developments shaped the administrative record. First, Dr. Corey Burchman — a New Hampshire board-certified anesthesiologist and pain management physician who practiced until 2019 — testified that once medical cannabis became available at Dartmouth-Hitchcock Medical Center, he and colleagues would 'avidly use that ability to limit opioids,' with some patients fully transitioning off prescription painkillers. He called cannabis 'extremely helpful in chronic pain patients as a means of analgesia.' Second, and more legally consequential: opponents cross-examined FDA's Dominic Chiapperino about the government's switch from a prior five-part test to a new two-part test for determining 'currently accepted medical use' (CAMU) — the CAMU determination is the legal keystone that classifies marijuana as Schedule III rather than Schedule I. An attorney for the states of Idaho, Indiana, and Nebraska pressed Chiapperino about a 2015 FDA analysis that concluded marijuana did not have CAMU, noting the new two-part test did not exist when that analysis began. Chiapperino acknowledged the new two-part test was introduced in July 2023 — just two months before FDA analysts finished the current analysis. The DOJ's OLC determined in 2024 that the prior five-part test was 'impermissibly narrow,' but opponents argued the midstream methodology change was improper. The government's ALJ attorney said the hearing was not the forum to litigate that dispute. The seven anti-rescheduling hearing participants all had the right to cross-examine the government's witnesses.
items.whyItMatters: The two-part CAMU test controversy is the opponents' sharpest line of attack — it challenges not the policy outcome but the legal methodology used to reach it, which is the kind of procedural argument federal courts evaluate under APA 'arbitrary and capricious' review. If the D.C. Circuit concludes the government improperly changed its CAMU methodology mid-analysis, it could vacate or stay the April 28 Schedule III order. Oklahoma OMMA operators' immediate compliance posture is unchanged: window closed June 29; operators who filed hold safe-harbor; those who did not should apply now via Form 225 at deadiversion.usdoj.gov; OBNDD enforcement begins January 1, 2027. However, compliance directors should monitor the DOJ's July 2 D.C. Circuit response brief for how the government defends the two-part CAMU test switch — this will signal the litigation strength of the April 28 order going forward.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — Opponents Attack Government Analysis Behind Rescheduling Recommendation on Second Day of DEA Hearing

items.headline: DOJ Files D.C. Circuit Response Brief July 2 — Challengers 'Come Nowhere Near' Stay Standard; 'Pocketbook Interests' Attack on NDASA/MMJ Standing
items.summary: The U.S. Department of Justice filed its response brief in consolidated D.C. Circuit rescheduling challenges (Cases 26-1106/1130/1136) on July 2, 2026, opposing petitioners' stay and vacatur motions. Three key legal arguments: (1) STAY STANDARD — Challengers 'come nowhere near' meeting the four-factor standard required for a stay. (2) STANDING — DOJ argued NDASA failed to establish Article III standing, offering only 'generalized speculation about how the rescheduling order might affect the drug-testing industry rather than particularized allegations about how the order has affected specific members of the association.' (3) ZONE OF INTERESTS — DOJ argued petitioners' alleged injuries fall outside the CSA's purpose: Congress enacted the law to protect public health and ensure proper regulation for medical/scientific purposes, 'not to guarantee revenue for drug screeners or protect future market opportunities for cannabinoid-based pharmaceuticals.' DOJ characterized petitioners as invoking 'pocketbook interests served by keeping all marijuana in schedule I.' The D.C. Circuit can now rule on the stay motion at any time following this brief.
items.whyItMatters: The DOJ brief is the government's substantive legal defense of the April 28 Schedule III order in the D.C. Circuit — the forum where the most consequential risk to Oklahoma operators lies. The strong stay-standard framing ('nowhere near'), standing challenge, and 'pocketbook interests' characterization collectively represent a comprehensive government defense of the order. A denied stay means the April 28 order remains in effect with Oklahoma operators' Schedule III status, 280E relief, and DEA registration intact. A granted stay would revert to Schedule I — but DOJ's brief signals strong government confidence in the order's durability. The D.C. Circuit's ruling on the stay motion, which could come at any time, is now the single most consequential pending legal event for Oklahoma operators.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — DOJ Marijuana Lawsuit Filing Cites Drug Testing Industry And Pharma Company 'Pocketbook Interests' In Opposing Rescheduling

secondarySource.name: The Marijuana Herald — DOJ Urges Appeals Court to Reject Attempt to Block Marijuana Rescheduling Order

items.headline: OMMA Launches Quality Assurance Lab Webpage — In-House State Testing Capacity Operational; Authorized by SB 813 (2023)
items.summary: The Oklahoma Medical Marijuana Authority launched a dedicated Quality Assurance Laboratory (QA Lab) webpage on June 29, 2026, signaling that OMMA's in-house QA Lab — authorized by Senate Bill 813 (2023) — is operational. The lab integrates advanced testing capabilities directly into Oklahoma's regulatory framework, reducing reliance on third-party testing data and increasing OMMA's capacity to detect improper testing practices, reporting errors, and product safety risks. OMMA recently hosted the Cannabis Regulators Association (CANNRA) for a lab tour. OMMA will update the webpage as pending accreditations are finalized.
items.whyItMatters: OMMA's in-house QA Lab changes the enforcement calculus for Oklahoma operators: OMMA can now independently re-test products and cross-check third-party lab results rather than relying solely on licensed labs' reported data. This directly addresses the testing integrity failures that drove the Greenleaf Labs recall (approximately 19,000 samples, April 2023–July 2025) and signals elevated OMMA enforcement capability on testing fraud. Cultivators and processors should expect OMMA to use QA Lab results to identify discrepancies and initiate enforcement actions against operators whose products carry inaccurate certifications. The lab's June 29 launch was overshadowed by the simultaneous opening of the ALJ broader-rescheduling hearing.

source.name: OMMA — Launches New Webpage Highlighting Quality Assurance Laboratory (June 29, 2026)

items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 4 (July 2): NDASA Testifies Rescheduling Eliminates Federal DOT Drug-Testing Authority for Safety-Sensitive Workers — Seeks Congressional 'Safety Carveout'
items.summary: NDASA (National Drug and Alcohol Screening Association), one of seven designated anti-rescheduling participants in the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing, testified today July 2, 2026 at DEA headquarters in Arlington, VA. NDASA's pre-filed core argument: moving marijuana from Schedule I to Schedule III would eliminate the federal government's legal authority to drug-test safety-sensitive transportation workers — pilots, truck drivers, air traffic controllers, railroad workers, school bus drivers — for THC. The legal basis: President Reagan's 1986 Executive Order granted HHS authority to certify workplace drug-testing procedures only for Schedule I/II substances; a 1991 federal statute requires all DOT-mandated drug testing to use HHS-certified labs and guidelines. Removing cannabis from Schedule I/II would remove it from HHS's testing jurisdiction and, by extension, eliminate DOT's authority to include THC in mandatory testing panels for safety-sensitive workers. NDASA called former DOT official Patrice Kelly as a supporting expert witness and is urging Congress to pass a statutory 'safety carveout' preserving DOT drug-testing authority independent of the scheduling outcome. Note: actual in-hearing testimony content is unverifiable until DEA releases the official post-hearing transcript (post-July 15); this item reflects NDASA's publicly pre-filed arguments and press statements.
items.whyItMatters: For Oklahoma OMMA-licensed cultivators and dispensaries, DOT drug-testing policy does not directly govern cannabis cultivation or retail operations. However, NDASA's transportation safety argument is one of the most legally distinct claims in the ALJ administrative record — it raises a concrete statutory conflict (Reagan EO + 1991 law) rather than solely a policy or public-health objection. This argument may attract bipartisan Congressional support post-hearing; if Congress passes a 'safety carveout' amendment as a condition of broader Schedule III rescheduling, it establishes a precedent of Congress attaching conditions to cannabis regulatory milestones. Compliance directors should also note that today's July 3 holiday recess means the next testimony day is July 6 (SAM / Kevin Sabet), not tomorrow.

source.name: InOut Labs — NDASA Press Conference on Marijuana Rescheduling and DOT Drug Testing

items.headline: State-Licensed Cannabis Operators Intervene in D.C. Circuit to Defend Rescheduling — First Industry Voices on Government's Side in Cases 26-1106/1130/1136
items.summary: MedPharm Iowa, LLC (d/b/a Bud & Mary's, Iowa) and Tri-Mountain Pure, LLC (Pennsylvania) filed a motion on June 29, 2026 seeking leave to intervene as respondents on the government's side in consolidated D.C. Circuit rescheduling challenges (Cases 26-1106/1130/1136). Both companies had already applied for DEA registrations under the expedited pathway. Their motion argues the federal government could not 'adequately represent' their interests and that they would suffer at least five distinct harms if the litigation blocks rescheduling — including loss of 280E tax relief, loss of federal registration benefits, and operational and competitive harm. The filing was made the same day the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing opened at DEA headquarters in Arlington, VA; it was first reported in Marijuana Moment's July 2, 2026 newsletter.
items.whyItMatters: This is the first time state-licensed cannabis operators have entered the D.C. Circuit consolidated challenges as active legal defenders of the April 28 order — joining DOJ on the respondent side. Oklahoma cultivators and dispensaries who filed DEA registrations under the expedited window have the same economic and regulatory stake as these Iowa and Pennsylvania operators: loss of 280E relief, loss of safe-harbor registration status, and operational disruption if the order is stayed or vacated. The D.C. Circuit must decide whether to grant the motion; if granted, it would add harm evidence and commercial-impact arguments to the legal defense beyond what DOJ alone presents. The April 28 order remains in effect — no stay has been granted as of July 4, 2026. The intervention filing and the DOJ's strong July 2 response brief (arguing challengers 'come nowhere near' the stay standard) together signal the legal defense of the order is broadening on multiple fronts.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — Marijuana Companies Seek To Defend Trump's Rescheduling Move By Intervening In Opponents' Lawsuits

secondarySource.name: Yahoo Finance — State Licensed Cannabis Companies Move To Intervene In MMJ's D.C. Circuit Litigation

items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 5 (July 6): SAM/Sabet Testified as Scheduled; Day 6 (July 7): DUID Victim Voices Today — Week 1 Cross-Exam Established FDA Didn't Interview Cannabis-Psychosis or Crash Families
items.summary: Day 5 (July 6, 2026): Kevin Sabet (CEO, Smart Approaches to Marijuana) testified as a designated ALJ hearing participant per Chief ALJ Julius's June 24 published schedule. Formal testimony content is under official blackout until DEA releases the post-hearing transcript after July 15. MMJ International Holdings issued a Newswire press release on July 6 headlined 'Harvard Expert Testifies State Dispensary Cannabis Does Not Meet Pharmaceutical Standards' — MMJ International is a D.C. Circuit litigation petitioner (adverse party to the April 28 order); this release is interested-party advocacy, not verified journalism; the claim does not constitute a regulatory finding. Day 6 (July 7, TODAY): DUID Victim Voices is testifying per the published schedule. WEEK 1 CROSS-EXAMINATION DETAIL (confirmed by Cannabis Business Times): DUID Victim Voices' attorney Patrick Kenneally (Burke Law Group) cross-examined FDA's Dominic Chiapperino during Week 1, establishing on cross that FDA did not interview parents whose children suffered cannabis-related psychosis — nor family members of car accident victims where the driver may have been cannabis-impaired — during FDA's 2023 CAMU scientific and medical evaluation. This is a documented procedural gap in the government's evidentiary record that goes to the completeness of the CAMU analysis.
items.whyItMatters: The Week 1 cross-examination detail — FDA's CAMU analysis excluded cannabis-psychosis families and traffic-accident families as data sources — adds a second evidentiary attack to the Day 2 CAMU five-to-two-part methodology challenge. Together, the opponents are building an administrative record that argues the CAMU finding underlying Schedule III classification was both procedurally incomplete (wrong methodology, missed data sources) and substantively flawed. For Oklahoma operators, the hearing scope remains ONLY adult-use/recreational marijuana per Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 order; state-licensed medical marijuana's Schedule III status is not at issue. MMJ International's Newswire 'Harvard Expert' release is being widely syndicated to look like neutral journalism — it is not; see discourse item watch-mmj-intl-newswire-as-neutral-hearing-journalism. Hearing blackout continues through July 15; the official DEA.gov transcript is the only authoritative record.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — Prohibitionists Punch Back at Cannabis Rescheduling Hearing

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Moment — Inside DEA's Cannabis Rescheduling Hearing (Newsletter July 6, 2026)

items.headline: White House Asks Congress to Delay or Revise November 2026 Hemp THC Ban — House Farm Bill Passed Without Delay; Hemp Planting Predictability Act Pending
items.summary: OMB Director Russell Vought sent a letter to House Speaker Mike Johnson on June 24, 2026 formally requesting that Congress either revise the hemp definition in Section 781 of P.L. 119-37 or delay the November 12, 2026 effective date that will ban hemp-derived products exceeding 0.3% total THC. The House Farm Bill (H.R. 8646) passed 224–200 on April 30, 2026 WITHOUT including a hemp ban delay or definition revision. The Hemp Planting Predictability Act (S.3686 / H.R.7024) — which would extend the implementation deadline to November 12, 2028 — has bipartisan sponsors in both chambers (Senate: Klobuchar D-MN, Paul R-KY, Merkley D-OR; House: Rep. Baird R-IN) but has received no committee hearing as of July 2026. The White House OMB request is a legislative communication, not a regulatory action, enforcement moratorium, or amendment to P.L. 119-37.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma dispensaries and hemp operators: Plan for November 12, 2026 compliance. The White House OMB letter signals executive-branch concern about the hemp ban's impact but does not delay or suspend the effective date of Section 781. The House already declined to include a delay in the Farm Bill. Standalone delay legislation exists (Hemp Planting Predictability Act) but has no committee hearing. A delay is politically possible — bipartisan sponsors, White House support — but not assured. The Senate Farm Bill markup is the most likely vehicle; monitor Senate Agriculture Committee activity. Until Congress acts or a federal court enjoins the provision, the November 12, 2026 effective date stands. Oklahoma hemp operators should begin reformulation, product transition, and inventory planning now.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — White House Asks Congress to Revise Hemp Definition, Delay THC Product Ban

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Moment — House Passes Farm Bill Without Hemp Ban Delay

items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 7 (July 8): AP Wire Confirms Kenneth Finn MD Called State Medical Marijuana 'Somewhat of a Ruse' — 18/27 Pain RCTs Showed No Benefit; Hearing Recessed July 9 Ahead of Schedule
items.summary: Kenneth Finn MD (Colorado-based pain management physician, co-vice president of the International Academy on the Science and Impacts of Cannabis) testified on Day 7 (July 8, 2026) of the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing at DEA headquarters in Arlington, VA. AP wire reporting from inside the hearing room — verified journalism from a reporter physically present — confirms Finn called state medical marijuana 'somewhat of a ruse' and testified that 18 of 27 randomized controlled trials examining cannabis for pain showed no benefit. After Day 7 concluded, the hearing recessed July 9 earlier than anticipated; no testimony occurred July 9. Day 8 resumes July 10 with the Tennessee Bureau of Investigation (TBI). Finn is a designated anti-rescheduling hearing participant — he is testifying as an advocate, not a neutral scientific referee. The official DEA.gov transcript (post-July 15 only) is the authoritative record; AP wire captures in-room journalist observation.
items.whyItMatters: AP wire reporting carries more weight than unverified social media claims, but is not the official DEA transcript. The 'ruse' characterization is now circulating as proof DEA will revoke Schedule III registrations issued to Oklahoma and other state-licensed operators — this interpretation is incorrect. Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 scope order explicitly confirmed the hearing addresses ONLY adult-use/recreational marijuana; Oklahoma state-licensed medical marijuana's Schedule III status is not at issue. Oklahoma operators' OBNDD registration obligation, 280E relief, and Schedule III status are unchanged regardless of Finn's testimony.

source.name: DEA — Marijuana Rescheduling Regulatory Actions (official transcript post-July 15)

secondarySource.name: Associated Press — DEA Rescheduling Hearing Day 7 Coverage

items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 5 (July 6): SAM's Harvard Expert Dr. Madras Acknowledged Under DEA Cross That Marijuana Meets Schedule III Criteria — Marijuana Herald In-Person Report
items.summary: The Marijuana Herald, whose correspondent was physically present at the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing, reported that Dr. Bertha K. Madras — a Harvard Medical School neuroscientist appearing on behalf of Smart Approaches to Marijuana (SAM) — acknowledged under cross-examination by DEA government counsel that marijuana meets Schedule III criteria. This concession is notable because Madras testified as an opposition witness; under adversarial cross-examination by the government, she acknowledged the scheduling criteria she was appearing to challenge. The official DEA.gov transcript, to be released after July 15, is the authoritative record; this item captures in-person journalist reporting of the cross-examination exchange.
items.whyItMatters: An opposition expert acknowledging under government cross-examination that a key scheduling criterion is met is a significant moment in the administrative record. Both sides are weaponizing this fragment: pro-reform frames it as 'even opponents admitted Schedule III'; anti-reform characterizes Madras's separate CAMU methodology challenge as a distinct argument not negated by the cross-examination acknowledgment. Oklahoma operators should not treat either spin as determinative; full context requires the post-July 15 official transcript. The April 28 Schedule III order for state-licensed medical marijuana is not at issue in this hearing per Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 scope order.

source.name: Marijuana Herald — In-Person Coverage of ALJ Hearing Day 5 (July 6, 2026)

items.headline: American Bankers Association Formally Endorses SAFER Banking Act — Sends Letter to Congress Urging Passage (July 7, 2026)
items.summary: The American Bankers Association (ABA) sent a formal letter to Congressional leadership on July 7, 2026 urging passage of the SAFER Banking Act (S.4942 / H.R.9471), which would bar federal regulators from penalizing financial institutions for serving state-legal cannabis businesses and explicitly extends safe harbor to ancillary businesses including landlords, technology vendors, accounting firms, and security providers. ABA is the primary trade association for the U.S. banking industry, representing banks of all sizes. The SAFER Banking Act was reintroduced with bipartisan support on June 25, 2026 (Senate: Merkley D-OR, Murkowski R-AK, Warren D-MA, Daines R-MT; House: Himes D-CT, Davidson R-OH, and others).
items.whyItMatters: ABA's institutional endorsement adds establishment banking-industry credibility to the SAFER Banking push. Oklahoma dispensaries and cultivators continue to face banking access challenges — accounts closed without notice, cash-only operations, limited payment processing. ABA's letter does not enact the law; Senate passage remains the key obstacle (the bill has cleared the House multiple times). However, formal ABA backing increases the bill's political viability in a Congress already engaged on cannabis rescheduling. Oklahoma operators should not change banking arrangements based on an endorsement letter alone.

source.name: American Bankers Association — SAFER Banking Act Endorsement Letter (July 7, 2026)

items.headline: Senate Agriculture Committee Farm Bill Markup Window July 13 – ~August 7, 2026 — Last 2026 Vehicle for Hemp Ban Delay; Senate Draft Does Not Include Delay
items.summary: The Senate Agriculture Committee's markup window for the 2026 Farm Bill runs July 13 through approximately August 7, 2026 — the last viable 2026 legislative vehicle for inserting a delay or revision to the November 12, 2026 federal hemp THC ban (Section 781 of P.L. 119-37). The House Farm Bill (H.R. 8646) passed April 30, 2026 WITHOUT a hemp ban delay. The Senate draft circulating as of July 2026 reportedly does not include a hemp ban delay. The White House OMB Director Vought formally requested Congress delay or revise the hemp definition in a June 24 letter to Speaker Johnson — executive-branch support for delay exists but has not translated to legislative text. The standalone Hemp Planting Predictability Act (S.3686) has bipartisan sponsors but has received no committee hearing and remains dormant.
items.whyItMatters: The Senate markup window is the last realistic opportunity in 2026 to attach a hemp ban delay to moving legislation. With both the House-passed Farm Bill and the Senate draft reportedly excluding a delay, the probability of legislative relief before November 12 is decreasing. Oklahoma operators selling hemp-derived Delta-8, Delta-10, THCA-flower, or other products exceeding 0.3% total THC (including THCA) must plan for November 12, 2026 compliance now — treat any Senate markup delay as a windfall, not a planning assumption.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — White House Asks Congress to Revise Hemp Definition, Delay THC Product Ban

items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 8 (July 10): TBI's Erica Stephens Argues Rescheduling Proposal is 'Deficient' — Eight-Factor Analysis 'Overlooks or Ignores Substantial Evidence'; Hearing Recesses Through July 12
items.summary: Erica Stephens, assistant special agent in charge of the Tennessee Dangerous Drugs Task Force, testified for the Tennessee Bureau of Investigation (TBI) on Day 8 (July 10, 2026) of the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing at DEA headquarters in Arlington, VA. Stephens argued the government's marijuana rescheduling proposal is 'deficient' and 'overlooks or ignores substantial evidence relevant to the required eight-factor analysis that weighs strongly against rescheduling marijuana to Schedule III.' Her testimony focused on how deregulation of marijuana — including the legalization of hemp — makes TBI's law enforcement mission more difficult, encourages involvement of criminal organizations in marijuana production and distribution, and negatively impacts the health and safety of Tennesseans. The hearing is now on weekend recess through July 12; it reconvenes Monday July 13 with Phillip A. Drum PharmD, then the state coalition (Nebraska, Idaho, Indiana) on July 14, before concluding July 15. Official DEA.gov transcript releases post-July 15 only — no verified detailed testimony content available until then.
items.whyItMatters: TBI's eight-factor adequacy challenge is the most procedurally direct attack on the rescheduling methodology entered into the administrative record so far: the argument is not simply that marijuana is harmful (a policy position) but that the government's formal CSA eight-factor scheduling analysis omitted or ignored substantial evidence — an administrative record challenge that, if credited by the ALJ, could form the basis for a recommended denial of Schedule III classification for adult-use marijuana. This is structurally similar to Day 2's CAMU methodology attack. For Oklahoma OMMA-licensed operators, the hearing scope is exclusively adult-use/recreational marijuana per Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 scope order; state-licensed medical marijuana's Schedule III status, OBNDD registration obligations, and 280E relief are not subject to the hearing and remain in effect. The remaining hearing schedule (Drum PharmD July 13, States July 14, conclusion July 15) will complete the opposition's evidentiary record; the ALJ's recommended decision on adult-use rescheduling will follow on an indeterminate timeline — historically, ALJ recommended decisions have taken two or more years. No stay of the April 28 order has been granted as of July 11, 2026.

source.name: Marijuana Herald — DEA Marijuana Rescheduling Hearing Pauses for Day After Moving Ahead of Schedule

discourse.headline: Consultants marketing paid 'DEA registration' services
discourse.claim: Consulting firms and law practices are selling paid services to prepare/file DEA Schedule III registrations, framing the 7-section form as too complex to self-file; some imply a single SOP error will delay federal approval.
discourse.caution: The portal is direct-to-applicant with a $794 fee paid to DEA; there is no official statement that paid representation is required. Counsel may be prudent for the Section 4 liability question, but be skeptical of vendors implying only they can navigate a self-registration process.

source.name: CannDelta

discourse.headline: Section 4 'have you distributed without DEA registration?' debate
discourse.claim: The application asks whether owners/operators previously manufactured/distributed a controlled substance without DEA registration — which nearly every existing cannabis operator must answer 'yes' to. DEA publicly said the question is 'not intended as a categorical barrier.'
discourse.caution: DEA's reassurance is informal — not published policy or rulemaking, not binding. Former federal prosecutors have warned against treating it as legal protection. Independent counsel before submitting is essential.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times

discourse.headline: June 22 deadline confusion: WRONG for manufacturers/cultivators; NOW CONFIRMED as dispensary portal closing date by credible sources
discourse.claim: RESOLVED AS OF JUNE 22: The June 22 date is now confirmed as the dispensary portal's own administrative closing date. Fennemore Craig ('Portal is Open Through June 22, 2026') and Foley Hoag LLP ('hard stop to registrations on June 22, 2026') both confirmed this. The June 22 date in Federal Register document 2026-08177 also referred to DEA notifying selected ALJ hearing participants — a separate use confirmed June 18–19. For manufacturers and cultivators using Form 225, the correct operative deadline is June 26 (last business day) / June 27 (last calendar day) — 2 days remain as of June 24. Previously, unvetted sites (HealthDataConsortium.org, hrzenden.com/Zen Den Co.) published 'June 22' as a universal deadline — they were wrong about scope but the date itself was correct for dispensaries.
discourse.caution: June 22 is confirmed as the closing date for the dedicated DEA dispensary portal. UPDATED JUNE 27: The 60-day window's 60th day falls on Saturday June 27; standard federal rules extend the effective filing deadline to MONDAY JUNE 29, 2026 — confirmed by Manatt, Phelps & Phillips. For CAKE as a cultivator/manufacturer, the operative deadline is now MONDAY JUNE 29 via Form 225. Dispensaries that missed the June 22 portal should contact DEA directly at deadiversion.usdoj.gov by Monday June 29. The June 22 confusion is resolved; the live question now is whether operators who haven't yet filed will use Monday's window. The Monday extension is a standard procedural rule, not a DEA formal extension.

source.name: Fennemore Craig — 'Portal is Open Through June 22, 2026'

discourse.headline: '280E relief applies whether or not you register with DEA'
discourse.claim: OMMA guidance and several sources assert 280E relief applies to all state-licensed medical operators by virtue of Schedule III, regardless of DEA registration — used by some to justify skipping registration.
discourse.caution: IRS/Treasury has not issued formal guidance; OMMA's statement is its interpretation, not a binding ruling. Some tax attorneys dispute it. Acting on it before Treasury guidance carries material tax risk. The DOJ's May 2026 lawsuit against TerrAscend demanding return of a pre-rescheduling 280E refund underscores IRS's aggressive posture on any 280E claims it views as unsupported.

source.name: OMMA notice / Marijuana Policy Project Q&A

discourse.headline: Claims the whole rescheduling/registration framework could be voided
discourse.claim: The SAM/NDASA D.C. Circuit petition, if it gets a stay, could void the Final Order and potentially unwind registrations filed under it; commentators warn operators are 'racing through a door that could be slammed shut.'
discourse.caution: Active litigation — OMMA itself warned all information 'remains subject to change pending the lawsuit's outcome.' Factor this into any large federal-compliance capital spend.

source.name: OMMA — SAM lawsuit update

discourse.headline: Vendors implying guaranteed or expedited DEA approvals
discourse.claim: Some marketing implies engaging a firm ensures approval or prevents delays; a Newswire PR item touts a vendor as having 'the answer' to DEA's registration questions.
discourse.caution: DEA makes registration decisions; no third party can guarantee outcomes or speed DEA's stated six-month review. Guarantees of approval or 'special access' are red flags. Paid PR is not verified reporting.

source.name: Newswire (paid PR)

discourse.headline: 'DEA registration unlocks interstate commerce' — now being promoted by named companies via press release
discourse.claim: Some consultants suggest DEA registration will enable interstate cannabis commerce. This claim escalated in May 2026 when LEEF Brands (a California cannabis company) issued a May 8, 2026 press release titled 'LEEF Brands Files Applications for DEA Registration, Positioning for Interstate and Global Export Opportunities,' framing registration as the gateway to selling across state lines and exporting internationally. The release was picked up by Cannabis Business Times.
discourse.caution: Interstate cannabis commerce is not authorized by the Final Order, the Federal Register rules, or any federal guidance. The DOJ order explicitly states registered operators remain 'subject to the limitations of their state license.' The treaty conflict with the 1961 Single Convention further blocks export pathways. LEEF Brands' release uses future-conditional language ('as regulatory frameworks evolve'), but the headline framing has been treated as current fact in trade coverage. Do not make Oklahoma business decisions premised on interstate commerce unlocking via DEA registration.

source.name: LEEF Brands / GlobeNewswire (May 8, 2026)

discourse.headline: Oklahoma operators confused over OMMA 'optional' vs OBNDD 'required'
discourse.claim: Local Oklahoma coverage and industry media show operators unsure whether to register now or wait for the manufacturer portal, and confused that OMMA calls dispensary registration optional while OBNDD calls manufacturer/distributor registration mandatory.
discourse.caution: These are different agencies governing different license types — not contradictory. Industry media is not a substitute for counsel. Much of the live conversation is in private Facebook/Slack operator groups not publicly indexed.

source.name: KXII (Oklahoma) coverage

discourse.headline: Operators warn: DEA registration is 'giving DEA a roadmap' to your operation
discourse.claim: Some industry veterans argue DEA registration creates permanent operational exposure beyond the Section 4 self-incrimination question — it documents facility location, scale, personnel with controlled-substance access, and supply-chain partners, and grants DEA on-demand unscheduled inspection authority. The concern is that previously extralegal operations are now fully mapped for federal enforcement.
discourse.caution: DEA's on-demand inspection authority is a statutory consequence of Schedule III registration under 21 U.S.C. 880, not unique to cannabis. Operators who fear this 'roadmap' risk should weigh it against the concrete risks of non-registration: OBNDD sanctions, loss of expedited safe-harbor, and potential license jeopardy. This is a legitimate risk factor for counsel to evaluate — not a verified basis for refusing to register.

source.name: Beard Bros Pharms — Oklahoma registration concerns

discourse.headline: MMJ International press campaign claims June 29 hearing unconstitutional — 'DELAYED AGAIN!'; hearing remains on schedule with all-opponent participant list released
discourse.claim: Since May 18, 2026, MMJ International Holdings has run an escalating public press campaign with alarming headlines: 'DELAYED AGAIN! Will Marijuana Rescheduling Survive Constitutional Challenges?' (May 26); 'DEA's Last Marijuana Gamble: Unconstitutional ALJ System Collapses'; and others via AccessNewswire and pressrelease.com. MMJ formally joined the D.C. Circuit challenge (cases 26-1106/1130/1136) on May 29, and on June 9 co-filed the stay motion. MMJ has also announced plans to seek emergency injunctive relief against the June 29 hearing itself on constitutional grounds (DOJ admitted DEA ALJs' removal protections are unconstitutional in MMJ BioPharma v. Bondi, Feb. 27, 2025). UPDATE June 18–19: DEA released the June 29 hearing participant list — all 7 selected participants oppose rescheduling; NORML and cannabis industry excluded. No court has granted any injunction or stay, and the hearing remains on schedule as of June 19, 2026.
discourse.caution: MMJ International is an interested litigation party — a pharmaceutical company that spent 7+ years pursuing FDA/DEA approval channels and now competes against newly-Schedule-III state-licensed operators. Its press campaign is advocacy, not journalism. The constitutional issue over DEA ALJ removal protections is real (DOJ conceded it), but it primarily affects the broader rescheduling hearing, not the April 28 state-medical-marijuana Schedule III order already in effect. Treat any claim that the June 29 hearing 'has been cancelled' or 'will definitely not proceed' as unverified until confirmed by the DEA or a federal court order. The risk of Oklahoma cultivators/processors not registering before June 26–27 due to MMJ's 'framework may be voided' narrative is concrete and immediate; the stay risk, while real, has not materialized.

source.name: PharmiWeb — June 29 Hearing Faces Constitutional Issues (May 18, 2026)

discourse.headline: Former federal prosecutor and IRS attorney warn: Do Not Rush DEA Registration
discourse.claim: A former federal prosecutor and a former IRS attorney are publicly advising cannabis operators NOT to file DEA registration before the June 27 expedited deadline without first consulting specialized criminal-defense and tax counsel. Arguments: (1) the Section 4 prior-distribution question documents conduct that was federally illegal when it occurred, and DEA's informal 'not a categorical barrier' statement is not binding policy; (2) the D.C. Circuit challenges could stay or vacate the entire order, making rushed registrations moot; (3) for operators whose primary concern is state-license continuity rather than federal supply-chain expansion, the registration risk may outweigh the benefit.
discourse.caution: This advice conflicts with OBNDD Director Anderson's directive that ALL Oklahoma OMMA manufacturers and distributors MUST register by ~June 27 to avoid OBNDD administrative sanctions up to license revocation. Unlike dispensaries (for whom OMMA calls registration optional), Oklahoma cultivators and processors face a state-level mandate from OBNDD — not merely a federal opportunity. Operators following this national commentary without accounting for Oklahoma's OBNDD requirement risk losing their OBNDD manufacturer registration and by extension their OMMA grower/processor license. Consult Oklahoma-licensed counsel familiar with both DEA registration and OBNDD requirements before deciding.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — Former Prosecutors Warn Against Rushing

discourse.headline: 'Federal rescheduling rewards a cannabis supply chain built on fraudulent safety testing' — MMJ International press campaign
discourse.claim: MMJ International Holdings (press release via AccessNewswire, June 3, 2026) claims the April 28 rescheduling order granted Schedule III status to a supply chain characterized by 'documented lab fraud, contaminated products, and a near-total absence of safety science' — without requiring a single state-licensed product to demonstrate safety, purity, or clinical efficacy. The company calls on federal courts and Congress to scrutinize the state-licensed supply chain before extending federal legitimacy. MMJ International is simultaneously a D.C. Circuit petitioner in Case 26-1136 and a co-filer of the June 9 stay motion.
discourse.caution: MMJ International Holdings is an interested litigation party — this is paid-wire advocacy, not independent journalism. Oklahoma has had documented lab-fraud cases (OMMA has acted on testing violations and issued product recalls involving fraudulent or failed lab results), so the underlying concern about testing integrity is not fabricated; however, the framing is designed to advance MMJ International's litigation strategy and commercial interests. As of June 19, no court has accepted this argument as grounds for a stay or vacatur. Oklahoma operators should monitor whether this argument gains traction in the D.C. Circuit, as a ruling that imposed additional safety-testing conditions on DEA registration would directly affect cultivators and manufacturers.

discourse.headline: Unvetted content-marketing websites publishing authoritative-sounding but unreliable DEA compliance guidance
discourse.claim: Multiple websites with no identifiable editorial standards, named authors, or organizational credentials are publishing DEA registration guidance that appears in Google results alongside legitimate law-firm content: HealthDataConsortium.org ('June 22, 2026: What Medical Cannabis Operators Must Do Now' — no author, no organizational affiliation found); hrzenden.com / Zen Den Co. ('DEA Registration Deadline Cannabis June 22' — apparent lead-generation content from a non-cannabis business); WeedPress 'The Paper Trail' (aggressive compliance-cliff framing, operated by an individual, targeting South Dakota operators but indexed broadly). discourse.caution: Oklahoma operators searching for DEA registration guidance are encountering these sites in search results, sometimes above credible law-firm or government content. Their articles may contain factual errors, be out of date, or be designed primarily for lead generation rather than compliance accuracy. No website is an authoritative source unless it ends in .gov. Cross-check any guidance against primary sources: the Federal Register notice (federalregister.gov/documents/2026/04/28/2026-08177), the DEA Diversion Control page (deadiversion.usdoj.gov), OMMA official bulletins (oklahoma.gov/omma), and OBNDD's director bulletin (obndd.ok.gov).

discourse.headline: 'The 280E Windfall May Leave Most Small Cannabis Businesses Behind' — compliance costs may outweigh tax savings for small operators
discourse.claim: WeedPress (May 31, 2026) argues that while large MSOs may benefit from 280E relief post-rescheduling, most small and mid-size cannabis operators will not realize the touted 'tax windfall' because: (1) DEA registration compliance costs (Section 11 cGMP requirements, legal fees for Section 4, $3,699/yr manufacturer fee) may exceed the actual tax savings for a small grow or processor; (2) DEA's six-month review period locks applicants in regulatory uncertainty; (3) the Section 4 self-incrimination exposure creates unavoidable legal costs; and (4) small operators who cannot meet Schedule III federal compliance expectations may face new federal exposure they did not have when operating in the pre-rescheduling legal gray zone.
discourse.caution: WeedPress is operated by an individual blogger with limited identified editorial credentials — it is not a peer-reviewed publication or established trade outlet. The underlying question (whether DEA registration compliance costs outweigh 280E savings for smaller operators) is legitimate and worth quantifying with your CPA and Oklahoma-licensed attorney. However, do NOT use this framing to conclude that DEA registration is optional for CAKE or other Oklahoma cultivators and processors — OBNDD's mandatory directive is not conditioned on whether the 280E tax benefit justifies the registration cost. The cost-benefit analysis is real; the conclusion that 'therefore don't register' is directly contradicted by OBNDD's enforcement position.

discourse.headline: Cannabis-industry attorney Jason Adelstone (Harris Sliwoski): D.C. Circuit challenge 'more likely than not' to stay and reverse rescheduling if standing established
discourse.claim: In a June 15–16, 2026 analysis distributed via AccessNewswire and the Canna Law Blog, Harris Sliwoski LLP attorney Jason Adelstone wrote that if the D.C. Circuit petitioners (NDASA, SAM, MMJ International, Nebraska, Indiana, and co-petitioners) establish standing and the court reaches the merits of the case, the DEA's marijuana rescheduling rule is 'more likely than not' to be stayed and ultimately reversed. Adelstone argued the petitioners' reliance on the D.C. Circuit's own precedent in NORML v. DEA may force the court to confront legal questions extending far beyond marijuana policy, and characterized the challenge as presenting a 'substantial threat' to the Schedule III rule.
discourse.caution: Harris Sliwoski is a reputable cannabis law firm and not an interested litigation party (unlike MMJ International), which lends this analysis more credibility than prior advocacy press releases. However, the key caveat is the 'IF petitioners establish standing' condition — standing is actively disputed and not guaranteed; the petitioners must first overcome standing hurdles before the court reaches the merits. Cannabis-industry attorneys have previously underestimated litigation risks in this space. More importantly for Oklahoma operators: this analysis does NOT change OBNDD's mandatory registration directive. The concrete risk of not registering by June 26–27 (OBNDD sanctions, potential license jeopardy) remains immediate and certain, while the stay risk — though real and now conceded by a cannabis-friendly attorney — has not materialized. Do NOT use this analysis as a basis to delay or forego DEA registration. Consult Oklahoma-licensed counsel who can assess both the OBNDD mandate and the litigation risk simultaneously.

discourse.headline: 'They're already at the door' — DEA inspection reports renew operator fears that registration triggers immediate federal on-site presence
discourse.claim: Following Marijuana Moment's June 19 report that DEA has begun on-site inspections at cannabis businesses that applied for Schedule III registration, operators are sharing reactions across industry forums and social media. The predominant narrative: registration has already triggered 5–6 federal agents arriving for six-hour site visits, confirming that applying is not a paper exercise but a direct invitation for DEA to map your facility, operations, and personnel. Some social commentary describes the Mississippi visits as a 'dry run' before DEA scales inspections nationally and warns that 'every dispensary that filed is getting a visit.'

The collaborative, inquisitive tone described by High Street Dispensary owner David Fowler does not support the interpretation that DEA is using inspections as pretextual enforcement. More critically for Oklahoma cultivators and processors: OBNDD Director Anderson has issued a mandatory directive to register — the risk of NOT registering by June 26–27 (OBNDD sanctions, potential OMMA license revocation) is immediate and concrete. Using inspection news as a reason to avoid filing directly contradicts OBNDD's mandate and does not eliminate the underlying compliance obligation. Operators genuinely concerned about inspection readiness should consult counsel and prepare documentation now — then file, not skip.

discourse.headline: 'DEA is formally extending the 60-day window' — still false as a regulatory action; but the Saturday rule DOES give Form 225 filers until Monday June 29

File Monday morning June 29 at deadiversion.usdoj.gov and retain timestamped confirmation. No further extension is anticipated.

discourse.headline: JUNE 27 — 'DEA extended the deadline to June 29' vs. the Saturday business-day rule — an important distinction now circulating
discourse.claim: Following Saturday June 27, operators and some cannabis media are describing the Monday June 29 effective filing window as 'DEA has extended the deadline' or 'DEA gave a 3-day extension.' These posts conflate the automatic federal Saturday-rule procedural shift with a formal regulatory extension of the 60-day window. The Monday window is confirmed real; the characterization as a 'DEA extension' is misleading.

No Federal Register notice has been issued. This distinction matters because: (1) Monday June 29 is confirmed and real for Form 225 filers; (2) no further extension beyond June 29 should be expected; (3) operators who hear 'DEA extended' and think there may be more flexibility are at risk of missing the actual final day. File Monday at deadiversion.usdoj.gov — June 29 is the hard end.

discourse.headline: 'The OBNDD January 2027 grace period means you have until January 2027 to file' — the most dangerous deadline confusion circulating as the expedited window closes
discourse.claim: Oklahoma cannabis operator forums, Reddit (r/OklahomaMarijuana, r/cannabiscultivation), and some local coverage are circulating the claim that since OBNDD announced a grace period until January 1, 2027 before administrative enforcement, operators have until January 1, 2027 to submit their DEA registration application. Some versions say 'we have six more months' or 'the real deadline is January.' This conflates two distinct deadlines.
discourse.caution: This is the single most consequential misunderstanding in the current landscape for Oklahoma operators. The OBNDD January 1, 2027 date is when OBNDD will BEGIN administrative enforcement actions (including potential license revocation) against operators who have not obtained DEA registration — it is not the date by which you must submit your application. The June 26, 2026 expedited window under 21 CFR 1301.13(k) is when you must FILE in order to: (1) maintain safe-harbor operating authority while your application is under review, and (2) qualify for DEA's six-month expedited processing commitment. An operator who files in December 2026 will have no legal safe harbor during DEA's review period and may not receive their registration before OBNDD's January 1, 2027 enforcement date. Missing the June 26 window does not give you more time — it eliminates your right to continue operating under state law while the federal application is pending. These are sequential deadlines, not alternatives. OBNDD Director Anderson's May 8 bulletin does not relieve operators of the June 26 filing requirement.

source.name: OBNDD Director Bulletin (May 8, 2026) — DEA Registration for Oklahoma OMMA Operators

discourse.headline: Simplifya (compliance software): 'Rescheduling Does Not Necessarily Mean Registering with the DEA' — dangerous framing for Oklahoma operators
discourse.claim: Simplifya, a cannabis compliance software company, published a piece on approximately June 19, 2026 arguing that DEA registration is not clearly mandatory and that operators should evaluate the cost-benefit before registering, given: (1) legal uncertainty around the rescheduling order, (2) unclear compliance cost burden, (3) the Section 4 self-incrimination problem on Form 225, and (4) six-month DEA review uncertainty. The piece concludes operators should consult counsel and may rationally choose not to register before the June 26 deadline.
discourse.caution: This argument may be appropriate guidance in states where regulators have NOT issued an explicit registration mandate. It is directly and dangerously wrong for Oklahoma. OBNDD Director Donnie Anderson issued an explicit bulletin on May 8, 2026 stating that ALL OMMA registrants who are manufacturers or distributors MUST obtain DEA registration, warning that failure 'could result in OBNDD administrative sanctions up to and including the potential revocation' of OBNDD registrations. Simplifya's framing is generic national advice that does not account for Oklahoma's state-level mandate. An Oklahoma cultivator or processor who reads Simplifya and concludes they can skip registration faces OBNDD sanctions that are entirely independent of whether the DEA rescheduling order is later stayed or vacated. The cost-benefit analysis Simplifya recommends is real — but in Oklahoma, the cost of NOT registering by June 26 includes loss of the OBNDD manufacturer registration that underlies the OMMA grower/processor license. Consult Oklahoma-licensed counsel, not national software vendor guidance.

source.name: Simplifya — Rescheduling Does Not Necessarily Mean Registering with the DEA

discourse.headline: 'The window closed — does that mean we can never register?' — post-deadline confusion circulating among operators who missed the June 29 expedited window
discourse.claim: Oklahoma cannabis operators and national industry forums are circulating questions and conflicting assertions about what happens now that the 60-day expedited window has closed: (1) 'If we missed the window, are we permanently barred from DEA registration?'; (2) 'Does missing the window mean automatic OBNDD license revocation?'; (3) 'Is there still a pathway or are we just done?'; (4) 'We heard the window might be extended because the dedicated portal never launched.'
discourse.caution: The expedited 60-day window closed June 29 — but DEA registration is NOT permanently closed. Operators who missed the window may still apply for DEA registration using Form 225 at deadiversion.usdoj.gov outside the expedited window. The consequences of missing the window are: (1) NO safe-harbor operating authority while DEA reviews the application — you are not protected during the review period; (2) OBNDD enforcement sanctions begin January 1, 2027 against operators who still do not have registration by that date; (3) there is no confirmed DEA extension of the window. Apply via Form 225 now if you have not already — late filing is better than not filing, but consult Oklahoma-licensed counsel about the gap period between application and registration when you have no safe harbor. No OBNDD automatic license revocation occurs on June 29 — enforcement begins January 1, 2027.

source.name: OBNDD Director Bulletin (May 8, 2026) — DEA Registration Grace Period

discourse.headline: Americans for Safe Access and Portfolio Media (Law360) join push to livestream June 29 hearing — no response from ALJ or DEA Administrator Cole as of June 28
discourse.claim: Americans for Safe Access (ASA) filed a formal letter to Chief ALJ Derek Julius requesting reconsideration of the ban on broadcasting or livestreaming the June 29 broader-rescheduling hearing; Portfolio Media (which publishes Law360) also joined the request, citing the public interest in real-time access to a 'historic' federal proceeding. These filings are in addition to the requests from Marijuana Moment counsel (June 23), Rep. Steve Cohen (June 27), and patient-advocate organizations. discourse.caution: These are procedural advocacy requests, not changes to the hearing's legal status. Chief ALJ Julius already stated he would not consider outside-party requests (Marijuana Moment, June 27). The escalation path to DEA Administrator Cole is the more direct route; if Cole overrules Julius before Monday 9:00 a.m., that would be an unusual and significant administrative move worth monitoring. Absent a reversal, the hearing is fully closed to remote observation — no livestream, no recording. The transcript release post-hearing is not a substitute for real-time monitoring if counsel needs to track specific testimony for compliance purposes.

source.name: Americans for Safe Access — Patient Advocates Ask ALJ to Reconsider Livestream Ban

discourse.headline: 'Stacked, Closed-Door Hearing' narrative dominant in cannabis media; ATACH argues selection contradicts Trump's own rescheduling stance; Harris Sliwoski warns one-sided record weakens legal durability

The ATACH angle (opposing Trump's position) is advocacy framing designed to pressure the administration and is not a legal argument. The Harris Sliwoski analysis on administrative record durability is legally coherent and worth monitoring in the D.C. Circuit context. For Oklahoma operators, no amount of post-hearing commentary changes OBNDD's mandatory registration directive or the June 29 Form 225 deadline. Do not let 'stacked hearing' framing create a false impression that the Schedule III order for state-licensed medical marijuana is itself under attack at the June 29 proceeding — Chief ALJ Julius's scope order is unambiguous.

discourse.headline: SAM's Sabet calls rescheduling 'solely political' on Day 1 — advocacy framing circulating in trade media as prediction of reversal
discourse.claim: Kevin Sabet (CEO, Smart Approaches to Marijuana), a designated hearing participant scheduled to testify July 6, issued a press statement outside DEA headquarters on June 29 calling the Trump administration's rescheduling 'solely political,' citing cannabis industry campaign donations (specifically Trulieve CEO Kim Rivers's donations to Trump inaugural committees and MAHA PACs). The government's own FDA witness (Chiapperino) entered substantive eight-factor scientific testimony into the administrative record on June 29, directly contradicting the 'solely political' framing. Sabet is scheduled to testify as a participant on July 6, meaning this framing will recur with new amplification on his testimony day. Oklahoma operators should evaluate this claim in the context of Sabet's litigation-adjacent advocacy role, not as objective legal or scientific fact. The April 28 Schedule III order remains in effect; no court stay has been granted.

discourse.headline: 'DOJ's nowhere near language means the stay is definitely denied' — premature certainty circulating post-July 2 brief
discourse.claim: Following the DOJ's July 2 D.C. Circuit response brief — which argued challengers 'come nowhere near' the stay standard and characterized their interests as 'pocketbook' commercial concerns — cannabis industry media and social channels are framing this as near-certainty that the D.C. Circuit will deny the stay and that the rescheduling order is now safe. Some outlets characterize the brief as a 'death blow' to the legal challenge. Simultaneously, anti-rescheduling commentators argue the DOJ's aggressive framing reveals political motivation and that courts have historically scrutinized treaty-authority-based agency actions tightly.
discourse.caution: The DOJ's response brief is one component of the D.C. Circuit's stay analysis — the court has not ruled. A strong government brief increases the probability of a stay denial but does not guarantee it; the court must independently evaluate each of the four stay factors (likelihood of success on the merits, irreparable harm, balance of equities, public interest) and threshold standing issues may be decided separately. Neither the 'death blow' framing (industry-friendly) nor the 'DOJ is desperate' framing (opposition) is legally grounded. Oklahoma operators should not change compliance posture — whether to apply for DEA registration, treat 280E relief as permanent, or make capital expenditure decisions — based on the content of the DOJ brief alone. The D.C. Circuit's actual ruling, when it comes, is the event to act on.

discourse.headline: 'Non-registered operators will be cut out of the supply chain' — DEA registrant-to-registrant purchase rule creating new pressure on late filers post-June 29

Operators who missed the June 29 expedited window and have not yet filed argue they face an immediate practical risk of being cut off from licensed supply-chain transactions — even before OBNDD begins administrative enforcement in January 2027. Some vendors are using this framing to urge late registration immediately.
discourse.caution: The DEA registrant-to-registrant purchase requirement under 21 CFR 1307.11 is a real legal rule, not merely industry commentary. However, active enforcement of this supply-chain restriction against Oklahoma state-licensed cannabis operators is not yet documented — its practical impact depends on how aggressively compliant supply-chain partners (distributors, dispensaries, processors) enforce it in day-to-day transactions. Using this framing to pressure peers into rushed or undiscounseled filings should be evaluated carefully. That said, this is a legitimate compliance risk: operators who have not yet applied should file via Form 225 at deadiversion.usdoj.gov now (late filing is better than not filing) and consult Oklahoma-licensed counsel about the no-safe-harbor gap period between application submission and final DEA registration.

No stay of the April 28 order has been granted as of July 11, 2026; Oklahoma operators' OBNDD registration obligations and 280E relief remain in effect. Do not adjust compliance posture based on hearing opposition testimony that is scoped exclusively to adult-use marijuana.
